description: Video recommendation algorithm based on readiness assessment results for Chapter 8: Weigh it, Pour it (Weight and Capacity)

excellent_performance.rationale: Student has excellent understanding of heavy/light, full/empty, and multiplication. Can fast-track to measurement concepts with challenge content

videoSequence.reason: Introduction to weighing and using a balance
Learn grams and kilograms as units of weight
Learn millilitres and litres for capacity
Challenge: Solve real-life weight and capacity word problems

ready_performance.rationale: Student is ready, provide complete coverage of weight and capacity concepts

partial_readiness.rationale: Student has gaps in heavy/light, full/empty, or multiplication. Needs targeted review before measurement concepts

adaptiveLogic.description: Select prerequisite videos based on specific weak concepts

exampleSequence.reason: Review: Comparing heavy and light objects
Review: Full, empty, and half-full containers
Start main topic: Introduction to weighing

not_ready.rationale: Student needs significant prerequisite review in comparing weight, capacity concepts, and multiplication before attempting measurement

videoSequence.reason: Foundation: Review comparing heavy and light objects
Essential: Learn full, empty, and comparing containers
Critical: Multiplication by 1000 for unit conversions
Begin main content: Grams and kilograms

postPlaylistAction.message: After watching these prerequisite videos, retake the readiness quiz to check if you are ready to continue with weight and capacity.

conceptBasedRemediation.description: Map specific weak concepts to remedial videos

heavy_light.conceptName: Comparing objects as heavy or light

full_empty.conceptName: Understanding full, empty, and half-full containers

comparing_quantities.conceptName: Comparing numbers and quantities using greater than, less than

comprehensionCheckpoints.description: Quick checks during video watching to adjust playlist

questions.question: Which unit would you use to weigh a mango?
questions.explanation: A mango is a small fruit, so we measure its weight in grams. A mango weighs about 200 grams.

questions.question: How many grams are there in 1 kilogram?
questions.explanation: 1 kilogram = 1000 grams. 'Kilo' means thousand, so kilogram means thousand grams!

ifAllCorrect.message: Excellent! You understand grams and kilograms well.

ifAllWrong.reason: Student struggling with weight units, review heavy and light basics

questions.question: How many millilitres are there in 2 litres?
questions.explanation: 1 litre = 1000 mL, so 2 litres = 2 x 1000 = 2000 mL.

ifCorrect.reason: Student ready for word problem challenges

ifWrong.message: Watch the conversion video again and practise converting with the rule: 1 kg = 1000 g and 1 L = 1000 mL.

skip_ahead.action: Skip practice video, jump to challenge or next topic

add_support.action: Pause main topic, insert prerequisite review on heavy/light and full/empty

suggest_break.trigger: After 4 videos watched in a single session
suggest_break.action: Suggest taking a 5-minute break before continuing

recommend_practice.trigger: Student completes videos but scores < 70% on checkpoint
recommend_practice.action: Recommend practising weighing and pouring with real objects at home

recommend_retake.trigger: Student in not_ready category completes all prerequisite videos
recommend_retake.action: Strongly recommend retaking readiness assessment before main content

high_score.message: Excellent! You understand heavy and light, full and empty, and your multiplication is strong. You are ready to learn about grams, kilograms, millilitres, and litres. We have created a fast-track playlist with challenge content for you.

good_score.message: Good work! You understand weight and capacity basics well. Let's learn about measuring with proper units like grams, kilograms, millilitres, and litres.

partial_score.message: You have some gaps in comparing weight or capacity. Don't worry! We will start with targeted review videos on the concepts you need, then move to measurement.

low_score.message: You need to review heavy/light, full/empty, and multiplication first. We have created a playlist focused on building a strong foundation. After watching, you can retake the quiz and then learn measurement.

conceptProgressionMap.description: Shows how weight and capacity concepts build on each other

concepts: Ordering objects by weight or capacity
Adding and subtracting with mixed units

playlistCompletion.description: % of students who watch >= 80% of recommended videos

practiceQuizPassRate.description: % of students who score >= 70% on practice quiz after watching playlist

videoRelevanceRating.description: Average student rating (out of 5) for playlist helpfulness

timeToMastery.description: Average minutes from readiness quiz to practice quiz pass

unitRecognition.description: % of students who can correctly identify whether to use g/kg or mL/L for a given object

conversionAccuracy.description: % of students who can correctly convert between kg-g and L-mL

implementationNotes.recommendedValidation: Test with 15-20 Grade 4 students across all readiness levels

expectedOutcomes.excellent_students: Complete in 40-50 minutes with 90-100% practice quiz score
expectedOutcomes.ready_students: Complete in 35-40 minutes with 80-90% practice quiz score
expectedOutcomes.partial_students: Complete in 50-55 minutes with 70-80% practice quiz score (after prerequisite review)
expectedOutcomes.not_ready_students: Require prerequisite review (25 min) + retake + main content (25 min) = 50-60 total minutes, expected 60-75% on practice quiz

commonChallenges.challenge1: Students confusing weight units (g/kg) with capacity units (mL/L)
commonChallenges.solution1: Use colour coding: weight in blue, capacity in green. Weight is for solid things, capacity is for liquids.
commonChallenges.challenge2: Students forgetting the conversion factor of 1000
commonChallenges.solution2: Use the memory trick: 'kilo means thousand' - so 1 kilogram = 1000 grams and 1 litre = 1000 millilitres.
commonChallenges.challenge3: Students struggling with mixed unit addition (e.g., 2 kg 300 g + 1 kg 700 g)
commonChallenges.solution3: Convert everything to the smaller unit first (grams or mL), add, then convert back to mixed units if needed.
